intro: Moving code from C++ to R switches the paradigm from high-performance systems engineering to domain-specific statistical computing and data analysis. R replaces manual algorithms and precise type definitions with high-level vectorized calculations, dynamic arrays, and native mathematical matrix tools. This transition accelerates data prototyping and statistical exploration at the cost of execution speed and memory predictability.

diffs.from: Static, rigidly compiled type structures with strict class mechanics, type coercion rules, and explicit memory offsets.
diffs.to: Dynamic, optionally typed, focused heavily on vectorized primitives (vectors, matrices, lists, data frames) and dynamic evaluation.

diffs.from: Complex imperative syntax requiring object specifications, block layouts, explicit pointers, and explicit iteration limits.
diffs.to: Expressive mathematical and functional syntax built entirely around implicit vector pipelines, function arguments, and dynamic attributes.

diffs.from: Explicit execution over stack structures, raw heap segments, and strict scope lifecycles using RAII mechanisms.
diffs.to: Automatically managed through a dynamic garbage collector, heavily utilizing copy-on-write semantics for complex data modifications.

diffs.from: Multi-threaded native environments utilizing std::thread, raw locks, condition variables, and parallel STL algorithms.
diffs.to: Primarily single-threaded execution context, relying on secondary parallel processing packages (parallel, future) or background worker processes.

diffs.from: Low-level structures providing fundamental containers, thread control, generic search layouts, and binary IO tools.
diffs.to: Deep statistical ecosystem providing built-in linear regression, distribution tools, plotting, and matrix manipulation primitives.

examples.title: Vector Element Multiplication
examples.description: Compares an explicit C++ loop multiplying array elements with R's native vectorized multiplication syntax.

examples.title: Linear Filtering Conditions
examples.description: Demonstrates conditional filtering using C++ algorithm loops versus R index mask filtering.

tips.title: Eliminate Explicit For-Loops
tips.body: Avoid translating C++ loops directly into R. For-loops are slow in R; always leverage vectorized functions or the 'apply' function family to let lower-level compiled code do the heavy lifting.

tips.title: Use Rcpp for Speed Bottlenecks
tips.body: If critical components suffer performance loss during migration, do not rewrite them completely. Use the 'Rcpp' package to embed legacy C++ code chunks directly inside your R workflows.

tips.title: Understand One-Based Indexing
tips.body: Beware array boundaries. C++ uses zero-based indexing (`0` to `N-1`), while R uses one-based indexing (`1` to `N`). Direct formula translations will offset outputs if not adjusted.

tips.title: Leverage Data Frames for Matrix State
tips.body: Replace complex C++ structures or multi-dimensional arrays with native R 'data.frame' or 'data.table' objects when managing heterogeneous tabular datasets.
